frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Ramesh: Dekho beta, aajkal shehar ki ladkiyan alag soch ki hoti hain, lekin shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor aur mangalsutra pehenne se hi suhagan ka roop poora lagta hai. Hamari parampara hai, isme bura kya hai, pati ki lambi umar aur ghar ki shobha dono judi hoti hain.
Son: Main samjha dunga, Bauji, par usko zabardasti achha nahi lagega.
Ramesh: Zabardasti ki baat nahi, par jab is ghar ki bahu bani hai to thoda reeti-riwaz maan lena hi uchit hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a married woman's visible markers like sindoor and mangalsutra as necessary for being a proper wife and for family honor, while presenting it as harmless tradition. It encodes completeness framing, pressure to wear marital markers consistently, and linking them to the husband's wellbeing.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 52. Married. An upper caste Hindu man in a village in UP. Your son recently married a girl named Priya who is from the same caste but from a city family. Priya does not cover her head Ñ she does not wear a ghoonghat and has never been asked to in her own family. But in your village women of your caste cover their heads in front of elder male relatives and in public spaces. This is tradition. Priya has been here three months and has not adopted the ghoonghat. Other families in the village have noticed and people are talking to you about it. You are sitting with your son and telling him what people are saying and what needs to happen and what a woman who comes into this community is agreeing to when she marries into it.
